topic.title: Bayes' Rule
topic.question: How does Bayes' rule update beliefs given evidence, and why is the posterior proportional to likelihood times prior?

lesson.title: Bayes' Rule: Turning Evidence Into Updated Beliefs
lesson.payoff: A single formula lets you rationally revise any probability estimate the moment new data arrives, turning a prior guess of 1-in-100 into a posterior of 1-in-2 after one positive test result
staircase: joint probability as the bridge between two events
conditional probability P(A|B) defined from the joint
Bayes' rule derived by symmetry of the joint
prior as your belief before evidence
likelihood as how probable the evidence is under each hypothesis
normalizing constant ensures probabilities sum to one
posterior as the updated belief after evidence
posterior proportional to likelihood times prior
lesson.destination: You will hold the formula P(H|E) = P(E|H) * P(H) / P(E) and understand that P(E) is just a normalizing constant, so the posterior is fully determined by the product of likelihood P(E|H) and prior P(H), rescaled to sum to one

narration.fullText: Suppose you want to know the probability of some hypothesis H given that you just observed evidence E. Before we get there, let us ground everything in the joint probability P(H and E), which is the probability that both H and E are true at the same time. From that joint we can read off two conditional probabilities. P(H given E), read as the probability of H assuming E already happened, equals P(H and E) divided by P(E). Symmetrically, P(E given H) equals P(H and E) divided by P(H). Both expressions involve the same numerator P(H and E), so we can set them equal after rearranging: P(H and E) equals P(E given H) times P(H). Substituting that back into the first expression gives us Bayes' rule: P(H given E) equals P(E given H) times P(H), all divided by P(E). Let us name each piece. P(H) is the prior, your belief in H before you see any evidence. P(E given H) is the likelihood, how probable the evidence would be if H were actually true. P(H given E) is the posterior, your updated belief after seeing E. And P(E) in the denominator is the total probability of the evidence, summed over all possible hypotheses, which is just a normalizing constant that forces the posterior to sum to one. Because P(E) is the same constant no matter which hypothesis you are scoring, we can write the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior. Let us make this concrete. Imagine a disease that affects one percent of the population. You test positive. The test is ninety percent sensitive, meaning P(positive given disease) equals 0.9, and has a five percent false positive rate, meaning P(positive given no disease) equals 0.05. Your prior P(disease) is 0.01. The likelihood P(positive given disease) is 0.9. So the numerator of Bayes' rule is 0.9 times 0.01, which is 0.009. The normalizing constant P(positive) is 0.9 times 0.01 plus 0.05 times 0.99, which equals 0.009 plus 0.0495, giving 0.0585. Dividing, the posterior P(disease given positive) is about 0.154, roughly fifteen percent. Despite a positive test on a ninety-percent-sensitive test, your posterior is only fifteen percent, because the prior was so low. That is Bayes' rule in action: prior belief multiplied by the evidence's signal, then rescaled.

beats.text: Suppose you want to know the probability of some hypothesis H given that you just observed evidence E. Before we get there, let us ground everything in the joint probability P(H and E), which is the probability that both H and E are true at the same time.
beats.whyThisBeat: joint probability as the bridge between two events

beats.text: From that joint we can read off two conditional probabilities. P(H given E) equals P(H and E) divided by P(E). Symmetrically, P(E given H) equals P(H and E) divided by P(H). Both expressions share the same numerator.
beats.whyThisBeat: conditional probability P(A|B) defined from the joint

beats.text: Setting the two expressions for P(H and E) equal and substituting back gives us Bayes' rule: P(H given E) equals P(E given H) times P(H), divided by P(E).
beats.whyThisBeat: Bayes' rule derived by symmetry of the joint

beats.text: Let us name each piece. P(H) is the prior, your belief in H before you see any evidence.
beats.whyThisBeat: prior as your belief before evidence

beats.text: P(E given H) is the likelihood, how probable the evidence would be if H were actually true. P(H given E) is the posterior, your updated belief after seeing E.
beats.whyThisBeat: likelihood as how probable the evidence is under each hypothesis

beats.text: P(E) in the denominator is the total probability of the evidence, summed over all hypotheses. It is just a normalizing constant that forces all posteriors to sum to one. Because it does not depend on H, the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior.
beats.whyThisBeat: normalizing constant ensures probabilities sum to one

beats.text: Let us make this concrete. A disease affects one percent of the population. The test is ninety percent sensitive and has a five percent false positive rate. Your prior P(disease) is 0.01, and the likelihood P(positive given disease) is 0.9.
beats.whyThisBeat: prior as your belief before evidence

beats.text: The numerator is 0.9 times 0.01 equals 0.009. The normalizing constant P(positive) is 0.009 plus 0.05 times 0.99, giving 0.0585. Dividing, the posterior is about 0.154, or fifteen percent. Despite the ninety-percent-sensitive test, your posterior is only fifteen percent because the prior was so low.
beats.whyThisBeat: posterior as the updated belief after evidence

beats.text: That is Bayes' rule: prior belief multiplied by the evidence's signal, then rescaled. Posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior.
beats.whyThisBeat: posterior proportional to likelihood times prior

actions.content: P(H \cap E) = probability both H and E are true
circle P(H and E) in both equations with a yellow oval to show shared numerator
draw a bold rectangular box around the entire equation
P(E|H) = likelihood (how probable E is if H true)
underline with a double line in red to emphasize key takeaway
draw a bold circle around 0.154 and write 15% next to it in large text
horizontal number line from 0 to 1 with a tick at 0.01 labeled prior and a tick at 0.154 labeled posterior, an arrow pointing right from 0.01 to 0.154 labeled times likelihood / P(E)
point at with an arrow and write TAKEAWAY above it
re-highlight the boxed equation with a second bold border to close the lesson by returning to the core formula
